1: Psalm 6 is traditionally the first of the Seven Penitential Psalms (Ps 6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143 — designated in early Christian liturgy, especially by Augustine and later the medieval church for use on Ash Wednesday and Good Friday). The Psalm assumes that the suffering being experienced is divine discipline. 'Do not rebuke me in your anger, nor discipline me in your fury' — the distinction is between two modes of divine correction: discipline under hesed (which restores relationship) and discipline under wrath (which may destroy the disciplined). The petition is not to escape correction but to receive it within the framework of covenant grace.
2: 'Heal me, O LORD, for my bones are in agony' — illness in ancient Israel was frequently interpreted as divine punishment for sin. This was not an idiosyncratic view but a widespread social-theological assumption: Job's three friends, the disciples' question about the blind man (John 9:2: 'who sinned, this man or his parents?'), and Deuteronomy's covenant blessings/curses (Deut 28:15-22: illness among the covenant curses for disobedience) all reflect this framework. Healing and forgiveness were functionally linked; requesting healing from YHWH was simultaneously requesting restoration of covenant relationship.
3: 'How long?' — the characteristic impatient question of the lament psalms, appearing throughout Book I: Ps 13:1-2 ('How long, O LORD? Will you forget me forever?'), Ps 35:17, Ps 79:5, Ps 80:4, Ps 89:46. The question is not despairing but covenant-bold: it presupposes that divine help is certain and that the covenant relationship makes the divine patron answerable to the client's need. The delay is intolerable not because God has abandoned the psalmist but because covenant faithfulness implies timely response. The question is permitted precisely because the relationship is real.
4: 'Turn back (shuva), O LORD, rescue my life; save me for the sake of your steadfast love' — shuva (return, turn, repent) is the primary Hebrew word for repentance toward YHWH: the prophets call Israel to 'return (shuvu) to the LORD' (Jer 3:1; Hos 6:1; Joel 2:12-13). Here the psalmist asks YHWH to be the one who 'returns' — a remarkable theological inversion where YHWH's restoration of favor is described in the same vocabulary as human repentance. The ultimate basis for rescue is hesed: not the psalmist's merit or even the depth of his repentance but YHWH's covenant commitment.
5: 'Among the dead no one remembers you; in Sheol no one praises you' — the pre-exilic Israelite understanding of Sheol (the realm of the dead) was of a shadowy existence cut off from covenantal worship and divine communication. This is not a denial of afterlife but a description of Sheol as the cessation of the covenant relationship's active dimension. The argument pressed to YHWH has a pragmatic edge: a dead worshiper is a lost worshiper. The living who can praise YHWH have more value to the divine patron than silent shades. This argument also appears in Hezekiah's prayer after his illness (Isa 38:18-19: 'Sheol cannot thank you; those who go down to the pit cannot hope for your faithfulness').
6: 'Every night I drench my bed with tears; I soak my couch with my weeping' — physical exhaustion from intense lamentation. Night-weeping was the recognized social expression of mourning in antiquity: Hezekiah wept on his sickbed as he prayed (2 Kgs 20:3); Jeremiah wept for Jerusalem (Lam 1:2: 'she weeps bitterly in the night, with tears on her cheeks'); Rachel weeping for her children (Jer 31:15) becomes the emblem of inconsolable grief. The Psalter's willingness to present the physicality of grief — soaked beds, wasted eyes — as the content of prayer treats bodily distress as a legitimate medium of communication with YHWH.
7: 'My eye has grown dim from grief; it wastes away because of all my foes' — in the ancient world the eye was the organ through which the soul communicated and was perceived. Wasted eyes signaled spiritual and emotional devastation (Lam 2:11; 5:17; 1 Sam 3:2: Eli's eyes growing dim as the word of YHWH became rare — spiritual and physical deterioration intertwined). The enemy's presence compounds the illness: the psalmist is under simultaneous pressure from physical deterioration and from those interpreting the illness as proof of divine rejection.
8: 'Depart from me, all you who do evil, for the LORD has heard the sound of my weeping' — the sudden transition from lament to confident dismissal, characteristic of the lament-to-praise movement in petition psalms. Some scholars identify this as the point at which the worshiper received a priestly 'salvation oracle' during temple liturgy — a pronouncement that the prayer had been heard, enabling the shift from petition to confidence. The weeping itself is presented as the content of the prayer: YHWH 'heard the sound of my weeping,' not the words alone. Paul's description of the Spirit interceding with 'groanings too deep for words' (Rom 8:26) resonates with this theology of non-verbal prayer.
9: 'The LORD has heard my supplication; the LORD receives my prayer' — three statements of divine responsiveness (v8: heard my weeping; v9a: heard my supplication; v9b: receives my prayer), each using a slightly different word for petition. The repetition is not redundancy but intensification: the certainty of divine reception is stated three times over. 'Supplication' (techinnati) carries the connotation of the dependent petitioning the patron for undeserved grace — the social dynamic of the client approaching the powerful benefactor.
10: 'All my enemies shall be ashamed and greatly disturbed; they shall turn back and be disgraced in an instant' — the anticipated shame of the enemies is the honor-shame reciprocal: they sought to shame the psalmist by interpreting his illness as divine rejection; YHWH's response reverses the shame onto the accusers. 'In an instant' (rega) — the speed of the reversal is itself a theological statement: when YHWH acts, the transformation is immediate and total. The enemies who interpreted the situation as confirming the psalmist's abandonment discover in the blink of an eye that they have misread it entirely.
